question_text: As the frequency of an ac circuit increases, the current first increases and then decreases. What combination of circuit elements is most likely to comprise the circuit? Inductor and capacitor. Resistor and inductor. Resistor and capacitor. Resistor, inductor and capacitor.
question_html:
As the frequency of an ac circuit increases, the current first increases and then decreases. What combination of circuit elements is most likely to comprise the circuit?

  1. Inductor and capacitor.
  2. Resistor and inductor.
  3. Resistor and capacitor.
  4. Resistor, inductor and capacitor.
solution_text: (a) and (d) are correct. In a circuit I max will be maximum when reactance is minimum. On increasing frequency in LCR circuit the reactance {tex}\left(X_{L}=2 \pi v L\right){/tex} due to inductance will increase and due to capacitance (X C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi vc}{/tex} ) it will decrease. So on increasing frequency X L . will be positive and x c will be negative for minimum reactance x L - x c must be zero. As reactance or impedance (Z) due to LCR series AC circuit is {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{l}-X_{c}\right)^{2}}{/tex} , Z will be minimum when X L - X C = 0 or X L = X C . So for given condition, X L and X C must be in circuit.
solution_html:
(a) and (d) are correct.

In a circuit Imax will be maximum when reactance is minimum. On increasing frequency in LCR circuit the reactance {tex}\left(X_{L}=2 \pi v L\right){/tex} due to inductance will increase and due to capacitance (XC = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi vc}{/tex}) it will decrease. So on increasing frequency XL. will be positive and xc will be negative for minimum reactance xL - xc must be zero. As reactance or impedance (Z) due to LCR series AC circuit is {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{l}-X_{c}\right)^{2}}{/tex}, Z will be minimum when XL - XC = 0 or XL = XC. So for given condition, XL and XC must be in circuit.

question_text: In an alternating current circuit consisting of elements in series, the current increases on increasing the frequency of supply. Which of the following elements are likely to constitute the circuit? Only resistor. Resistor and an inductor. Resistor and a capacitor. Only a capacitor.
question_html:
In an alternating current circuit consisting of elements in series, the current increases on increasing the frequency of supply. Which of the following elements are likely to constitute the circuit?

  1. Only resistor.
  2. Resistor and an inductor.
  3. Resistor and a capacitor.
  4. Only a capacitor.
solution_text: (c) and d) are correct. In this problem, the current increases on increasing the frequency of the supply. Hence, the reactance of the circuit must be decreased as an increase in frequency. So, one element that must be connected is a capacitor. We can also connect a resistor in series. For a capacitive circuit, {tex}X_C = \frac {1}{\omega C} = \frac {1}{2 \pi fC}{/tex} When frequency increases, X C decreases. Hence current in the circuit increases. R does not vary with frequency, therefore, likely elements constituting the circuit may be capacitor only or capacitor and a resistor. Choice (c ) and (d) are correct
solution_html:
(c) and d) are correct.
In this problem, the current increases on increasing the frequency of the supply. Hence, the reactance of the circuit must be decreased as an increase in frequency. So, one element that must be connected is a capacitor. We can also connect a resistor in series. For a capacitive circuit,
{tex}X_C = \frac {1}{\omega C} = \frac {1}{2 \pi fC}{/tex}
When frequency increases, XC decreases. Hence current in the circuit increases. R does not vary with frequency, therefore, likely elements constituting the circuit may be capacitor only or capacitor and a resistor. Choice (c ) and (d) are correct

question_text: Electrical energy is transmitted over large distances at high alternating voltages. Which of the following statements is (are) correct? For a given power level, there is a lower current. Lower current implies less power loss. Transmission lines can be made thinner. It is easy to reduce the voltage at the receiving end using step-down transformers.
question_html:
Electrical energy is transmitted over large distances at high alternating voltages. Which of the following statements is (are) correct?

  1. For a given power level, there is a lower current.
  2. Lower current implies less power loss.
  3. Transmission lines can be made thinner.
  4. It is easy to reduce the voltage at the receiving end using step-down transformers.
solution_text: (a), (b) and (d) are correct. The power is to be transmitted over the large distances at high alternating voltages, so current flowing through the wires will be low because of given power (P). For a given power level, we find that P = E rms I rms (I rms is low when E rms is high) Power loss = I 2 rms R = low ( {tex}\because{/tex} I rms is low) Now at the receiving end high voltage is reduced by using step-down transformers. A step down transformer lowers voltage by increasing current.
solution_html:
(a), (b) and (d) are correct.
The power is to be transmitted over the large distances at high alternating voltages, so current flowing through the wires will be low because of given power (P).
For a given power level, we find that
P = ErmsIrms(Irms is low when Erms is high)
Power loss = I2rms R = low ({tex}\because{/tex} Irms is low)
Now at the receiving end high voltage is reduced by using step-down transformers. A step down transformer lowers voltage by increasing current.

question_text: For an LCR circuit, the power transferred from the driving source to the driven oscillator is P = I 2 Z cos φ. Here, the power factor {tex}\cos\phi \geq 0, P \geq 0{/tex} . The driving force can give no energy to the oscillator (P = 0) in some cases. The driving force cannot syphon out (P < 0) the energy out of oscillator. The driving force can take away energy out of the oscillator.
question_html:
For an LCR circuit, the power transferred from the driving source to the driven oscillator is P = I2Z cos φ.

  1. Here, the power factor  {tex}\cos\phi \geq 0, P \geq 0{/tex}.
  2. The driving force can give no energy to the oscillator (P = 0) in some cases.
  3. The driving force cannot syphon out (P < 0) the energy out of oscillator.
  4. The driving force can take away energy out of the oscillator.
solution_text: (a), (b) and (c) are correct. In the given problem power transferred, {tex}P=I^{2} Z \cos \phi{/tex} Where I is the current, Z = Impedance and cos {tex}\phi{/tex} is power factor As power factor, {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z}{/tex} where R > 0 and Z > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \phi>0 \Rightarrow P>0{/tex} When {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (in case of L or C), P = 0 From (a), it is clear that P < 0 is not possible.
solution_html:
(a), (b) and (c) are correct.
In the given problem power transferred,
{tex}P=I^{2} Z \cos \phi{/tex}
Where I is the current, Z = Impedance and cos {tex}\phi{/tex} is power factor

  1. As power factor, {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z}{/tex}
    where R > 0 and Z > 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \phi>0 \Rightarrow P>0{/tex}
  2. When {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (in case of L or C), P = 0
  3. From (a), it is clear that P < 0 is not possible.

question_text: When an AC voltage of 220 V is applied to the capacitor C the maximum voltage between plates is 220 V. the current is in phase with the applied voltage. the charge on the plates is in phase with the applied voltage. power delivered to the capacitor is zero.
question_html:
When an AC voltage of 220 V is applied to the capacitor C

  1. the maximum voltage between plates is 220 V.
  2. the current is in phase with the applied voltage.
  3. the charge on the plates is in phase with the applied voltage.
  4. power delivered to the capacitor is zero.
solution_text: (c) and (d) are correct. If the alternating voltage is applied to the capacitor the plate connected to the positive terminal of the source will be at higher potential and the plate connected to the negative terminal of the source will be at lower potential. So the plates capacitor is charged. (c) If {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t, Q=C V_{0}\sin \omega t{/tex} or we can say that Q and emf are in phase and voltage changes due to sin factor hence discard option(a) (d) As P = V rms I rms {tex}\cos \phi{/tex} and in case of a capacitor, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} P = 0, or we can say that power delivered to the capacitor is zero. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P av = Power delivered = 0 As the circuit is pure capacitive so, the current developed leads the applied voltage by a phase angle of 90 0 hence discard option (b)
solution_html:
(c) and (d) are correct.
If the alternating voltage is applied to the capacitor the plate connected to the positive terminal of the source will be at higher potential and the plate connected to the negative terminal of the source will be at lower potential. So the plates capacitor is charged.
(c) If  {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t, Q=C V_{0}\sin \omega t{/tex} or we can say that Q and emf are in phase and voltage changes due to sin factor hence discard option(a)
(d) As P = VrmsIrms {tex}\cos \phi{/tex} and in case of a capacitor, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} P = 0, or we can say that power delivered to the capacitor is zero.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Pav = Power delivered = 0

As the circuit is pure capacitive so, the current developed leads the applied voltage by a phase angle of 900  hence discard option (b)

question_text: The line that draws power supply to your house from street has zero average current. 220 V average voltage. voltage and current out of phase by 90°. voltage and current possibly differing in phase {tex}\phi{/tex} such that {tex}|\phi|<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_html:
The line that draws power supply to your house from street has

  1. zero average current.
  2. 220 V average voltage.
  3. voltage and current out of phase by 90°.
  4. voltage and current possibly differing in phase {tex}\phi{/tex} such that {tex}|\phi|<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_text: (a) and (d) are correct. Alternating currents are used for household supplies, which are having zero average value over a cycle. The line is having some resistance, so power factor cos {tex}\phi=\frac{R}{Z} \neq 0{/tex} So, {tex}\phi \neq \pi / 2 \Rightarrow \phi<\pi / 2{/tex} i.e., phase lies between 0 and {tex}\frac {\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
(a) and (d) are correct.
Alternating currents are used for household supplies, which are having zero average value over a cycle.
The line is having some resistance, so power factor cos {tex}\phi=\frac{R}{Z} \neq 0{/tex}
So, {tex}\phi \neq \pi / 2 \Rightarrow \phi<\pi / 2{/tex}
i.e., phase lies between 0 and {tex}\frac {\pi}{2}{/tex}

question_text: If a L-C circuit is considered analogous to a harmonically oscillating spring block system, which energy of the L-C circuit would be analogous to potential energy and which one analogous to kinetic energy?
question_html: If a L-C circuit is considered analogous to a harmonically oscillating spring block system, which energy of the L-C circuit would be analogous to potential energy and which one analogous to kinetic energy?
solution_text: An L-C circuit is analogous to a harmonically oscillating spring block system. Therefore energy due to motion of charge particle i.e., magnetic energy {tex}\frac{1}{2LI^2}{/tex} is analogous to kinetic energy. The electrostatic energy due to charging of capacitor {tex}\frac{1}{2CV^2}{/tex} is analogous to potential energy.
solution_html: An L-C circuit is analogous to a harmonically oscillating spring block system. Therefore energy due to motion of charge particle i.e., magnetic energy {tex}\frac{1}{2LI^2}{/tex} is analogous to kinetic energy. The electrostatic energy due to charging of capacitor {tex}\frac{1}{2CV^2}{/tex} is analogous to potential energy.

Study the circuits (a) and (b) shown in Figure and answer the following questions.

  2. Can the rms current in circuit (b) be larger than that in (a)?
solution_text: Condition of the resonance is given by: Let us first assume, rms current in circuit A = (I rms )A And rms current in circuit B = (I rms )B {tex}\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{A}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{ms}}}{R}{/tex} {tex}\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{B}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{ms}}}{Z}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{ms}}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} When (Irms)A = (Irms)B {tex}R=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad X_{L}=X_{C}{/tex} resonance condition If Erms in the two circuits are same, then at resonance the arms current in LCR will be same as that in R circuit (circuit A). As {tex}Z \geq R{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left(I_{\mathrm{ms}}\right)_{B}}{\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{A}}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{ms}} / Z}{E_{\mathrm{ms}} / R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{Z} \leq 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(I_{\mathrm{ms}}\right) a \geq\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right) b{/tex} No, {tex}R \leq Z{/tex} . So, arms current in circuit (b) cannot be larger than that in (a).

Condition of the resonance is given by: Let us first assume, rms current in circuit A = (Irms)A
And rms current in circuit B = (Irms)B
{tex}\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{A}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{ms}}}{R}{/tex}
{tex}\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{B}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{ms}}}{Z}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{ms}}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}

  1. When (Irms)A = (Irms)B
    {tex}R=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad X_{L}=X_{C}{/tex} resonance condition
    If Erms in the two circuits are same, then at resonance the arms current in LCR will be same as that in R circuit (circuit A).
  2. As {tex}Z \geq R{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left(I_{\mathrm{ms}}\right)_{B}}{\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{A}}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{ms}} / Z}{E_{\mathrm{ms}} / R}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{R}{Z} \leq 1{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(I_{\mathrm{ms}}\right) a \geq\left(I_{\mathrm{rms}}\right) b{/tex}

question_text: Can the instantaneous power output of an ac source ever be negative? Can the average power output be negative?
question_html: Can the instantaneous power output of an ac source ever be negative? Can the average power output be negative?
solution_text: power is defined as the product of voltage and that component of the current which is in phase with the voltage. To proceed the question, let us assume the applied emf in the circuit containing L, C or a combination of L, C and R E = E 0 sin( {tex}\omega{/tex} t) Hence current developed is {tex}I=I_{0} \sin (\omega t \pm \phi){/tex} Instantaneous power output of the AC source P = EI = (E 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\left[I_{0} \sin (\omega t \pm \phi)\right]{/tex} {tex}=E_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}=\frac{E_{0} I_{0}}{2}[\cos \phi-\cos (2 \omega t+\phi)]{/tex} .... (i) Clearly, from eq. (i) When {tex}\cos \phi<\cos (2 \omega t+\phi){/tex} P < 0 So, yes, the instantaneoús power output of an AC source can be negative. Average power {tex}P_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{V_{0}}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{I_{0}}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}=V_{\mathrm{rms}} I_{\mathrm{rms}} \cos \phi{/tex} .... (ii) where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase difference, From eq. (ii) P av > 0 Because {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z} \geq 0{/tex} No, the average power output of an AC source cannot be negative.

power is defined as the product of voltage and that component of the current which is in phase with the voltage.  To proceed the question, let us assume the applied emf in the circuit containing L, C or a combination of L, C and R
E = E0 sin({tex}\omega{/tex}t)
Hence current developed is
{tex}I=I_{0} \sin (\omega t \pm \phi){/tex}

  1. Instantaneous power output of the AC source
    P = EI = (E0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t) {tex}\left[I_{0} \sin (\omega t \pm \phi)\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=E_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{E_{0} I_{0}}{2}[\cos \phi-\cos (2 \omega t+\phi)]{/tex} .... (i)
    Clearly, from eq. (i)
    When {tex}\cos \phi<\cos (2 \omega t+\phi){/tex}
    P < 0
    So, yes, the instantaneoús power output of an AC source can be negative.
  2. Average power {tex}P_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{V_{0}}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{I_{0}}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex}
    {tex}=V_{\mathrm{rms}} I_{\mathrm{rms}} \cos \phi{/tex} .... (ii)
    where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase difference,
    From eq. (ii) Pav > 0
    Because {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z} \geq 0{/tex}
    No, the average power output of an AC source cannot be negative.

question_html: In series LCR circuit, the plot of Imax vs {tex}\omega{/tex} is shown in Figure. Find the bandwidth and mark in the figure.
solution_text: The bandwidth in an LCR-circuit corresponds the frequencies at which, I m = {tex}\frac{I_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 0.707 I max In below figure, the dotted line parallel to {tex}\omega{/tex} -axis has been drawn corresponding to I m = 0.7 I max . The dotted line cuts the graph in the points A and B. It follows that corresponding to the points A and B, {tex}\omega_A{/tex} = 0.8 rad s -1 and {tex}\omega_B{/tex} = 1.2 rad s -1 Therefore, bandwidth is given by {tex}\Delta \omega = \omega_B - \omega_A{/tex} = 1.2 - 0.8 = 0.4 rad s -1
solution_html:
The bandwidth in an LCR-circuit corresponds the frequencies at which,
Im = {tex}\frac{I_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 0.707 Imax 
In below figure, the dotted line parallel to {tex}\omega{/tex}-axis has been drawn corresponding to Im = 0.7 Imax. The dotted line cuts the graph in the points A and B.

It follows that corresponding to the points A and B,
{tex}\omega_A{/tex} = 0.8 rad s-1 and {tex}\omega_B{/tex} = 1.2 rad s-1 
Therefore, bandwidth is given by
{tex}\Delta \omega = \omega_B - \omega_A{/tex} = 1.2 - 0.8 = 0.4 rad s-1

question_text: Both alternating current and direct current are measured in amperes. But how is the ampere defined for an alternating current?
question_html: Both alternating current and direct current are measured in amperes. But how is the ampere defined for an alternating current?
solution_text: For a Direct Current (DC), 1 ampere = 1 coulomb/sec The direction of AC changes with the frequency of source with the source frequency and the attractive force would average to zero. Thus, the AC ampere must be defined in terms of some property that is independent of the direction of the current. Joule's heating effect is such property and hence it is used to define rms value of AC. So, r.m.s. value of AC is equal to that value of DC, which when passed through a resistance for a given time will produce the same amount of heat as produced by the alternating current when passed through the same resistance for the same time.
solution_html:
For a Direct Current (DC),
1 ampere = 1 coulomb/sec
The direction of AC changes with the frequency of source with the source frequency and the attractive force would average to zero.
Thus, the AC ampere must be defined in terms of some property that is independent of the direction of the current. Joule's heating effect is such property and hence it is used to define rms value of AC.
So, r.m.s. value of AC is equal to that value of DC, which when passed through a resistance for a given time will produce the same amount of heat as produced by the alternating current when passed through the same resistance for the same time.

question_text: A coil of 0.01 henry inductance and 1 ohm resistance is connected to 200 volt, 50 Hz ac supply. Find the impedance of the circuit and time lag between max alternating voltage and current.
question_html: A coil of 0.01 henry inductance and 1 ohm resistance is connected to 200 volt, 50 Hz ac supply. Find the impedance of the circuit and time lag between max alternating voltage and current.
solution_text: According to the given problem inductance L = 0.01 H, resistance R = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , voltage (V) = 200 V and frequency (f) = 50 Hz {tex}X_{L}=2 \pi f L{/tex} {tex}=2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.01=3.14 \Omega{/tex} The impedance of the R-L circuit {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{R^{2}+(2 \pi f L)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1^{2}+(2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.01)^{2}}{/tex} thus impedance is equal to {tex}Z=\sqrt{10.86}=3.3 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{\omega L}{R}=\frac{2 \pi f L}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.01}{1}=3.14{/tex} {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}(3.14)=72^{\circ}{/tex} Phase difference {tex}\phi=\frac{72 \times \pi}{180} \mathrm{rad}{/tex} The time lag between alternating voltage and current, {tex}\Delta t=\frac{\phi}{\omega}=\frac{72 \pi}{180 \times 2 \pi \times 50}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{250} \mathrm{s}=0.004 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the given  problem inductance L = 0.01 H,
resistance R = 1{tex}\Omega{/tex}, voltage (V) = 200 V
and frequency (f) = 50 Hz
{tex}X_{L}=2 \pi f L{/tex} {tex}=2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.01=3.14 \Omega{/tex}
The impedance of the R-L circuit
{tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{R^{2}+(2 \pi f L)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1^{2}+(2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.01)^{2}}{/tex}
thus impedance is equal to {tex}Z=\sqrt{10.86}=3.3 \Omega{/tex}
{tex}\tan \phi=\frac{\omega L}{R}=\frac{2 \pi f L}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.01}{1}=3.14{/tex}
{tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}(3.14)=72^{\circ}{/tex}
Phase difference {tex}\phi=\frac{72 \times \pi}{180} \mathrm{rad}{/tex}
The time lag between alternating voltage and current,
{tex}\Delta t=\frac{\phi}{\omega}=\frac{72 \pi}{180 \times 2 \pi \times 50}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{250} \mathrm{s}=0.004 \mathrm{s}{/tex}

question_text: Explain why the reactance provided by a capacitor to an alternating current decreases with increasing frequency.
question_html: Explain why the reactance provided by a capacitor to an alternating current decreases with increasing frequency.
solution_text: When AC current is applied across a capacitor plate, the plates of the capacitor get charge and discharge alternately. Current through the capacitor is a result of this charging and discharging by AC voltage or current. A capacitor does not allow direct current (having zero frequency) to pass through it. But as the frequency of current increases capacitor will pass more current through it as on increasing frequency, the charging and discharging happen at a fast rate. It implies that the reactance offered by the capacitor decreases on an increasing frequency. So the reactance of a capacitor can be written as {tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex}
solution_html:
When AC current is applied across a capacitor plate, the plates of the capacitor get charge and discharge alternately. Current through the capacitor is a result of this charging and discharging by AC voltage or current.
A capacitor does not allow direct current (having zero frequency) to pass through it. But as the frequency of current increases capacitor will pass more current through it as on increasing frequency, the charging and discharging happen at a fast rate. It implies that the reactance offered by the capacitor decreases on an increasing frequency.
So the reactance of a capacitor can be written as {tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Explain why the reactance offered by an inductor increases with increasing frequency of an alternating voltage.
question_html: Explain why the reactance offered by an inductor increases with increasing frequency of an alternating voltage.
solution_text: The inductive reactance is given by X L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL, X L is proportional to the frequency and current is inversely proportional to the reactance. An inductor opposes the flow of current through it by developing a back emf according to Lenz's law. If the current is decreasing, the polarity of the induced emf will be so as to increase the current and vice-versa. Since the induced emf is proportional to the rate of change of current. Hence the reactance increases with frequency.
solution_html:
The inductive reactance is given by XL = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}fL, XL is proportional to the frequency and current is inversely proportional to the reactance. An inductor opposes the flow of current through it by developing a back emf according to Lenz's law. If the current is decreasing, the polarity of the induced emf will be so as to increase the current and vice-versa.
Since the induced emf is proportional to the rate of change of current. Hence the reactance increases with frequency.

question_text: Consider the LCR circuit shown in Figure. Find the net current i and the phase of i. Show that {tex}i=\frac{v}{Z}{/tex} . Find the impedance Z for this circuit.
question_html: Consider the LCR circuit shown in Figure. Find the net current i and the phase of i. Show that {tex}i=\frac{v}{Z}{/tex}. Find the impedance Z for this circuit.
solution_text: According to the above figure, the total current 'i' is divided into two parts i 2 through R and i 1 through a series combination of C and L. So, we get i = i 1 + i 2 As, {tex}V_{m} \sin \omega t=R i_{2},{/tex} [from the circuit diagram, we find that] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad i_{2}=\frac{V_{m} \sin \omega t}{R}{/tex} .... (i) Let q 1 is charge on the capacitor at any time t, i 1 is the current in the lower circuit. thus, by Applying KVL in the lower circuit as shown in the figure, {tex}V_{m} \sin \omega t-\frac{q_{1}}{C}-\frac{L d i_{1}}{d t}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{q_{1}}{C}+\frac{L d^{2} q_{1}}{d t^{2}}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} ..... (ii) Let {tex}q_{1}=q_{m} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} .... (iii) {tex}\therefore \quad i_{1}=\frac{d q_{1}}{d t}=q_{m} \omega \cos ^{}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\left(i_{1}\right)}{d t}=\frac{d^{2} q_{1}}{d t^{2}}=-q_{m} \omega^{2} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} Now putting these values in eq. (ii), we get {tex}q_{m}\left[\frac{1}{C}+L\left(-\omega^{2}\right)\right] \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} If {tex}\phi=0{/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{1}{C}-L \omega^{2}\right)>0{/tex} then {tex}q_{m}=\frac{V_{m}}{\left(\frac{1}{C}-L \omega^{2}\right)}{/tex} .... (iv) From Eq. (iii), {tex}i_{1}=\frac{d q_{1}}{d t}=\omega q_{m} \cos (\omega t+\phi){/tex} Using eq. (iv), {tex}i_{1}=\frac{\omega V_{m} \cos (\omega t+\phi)}{\frac{1}{C}-L \omega^{2}}{/tex} Taking {tex}\phi=0 ; i_{1}=\frac{V_{m} \cos (\omega t)}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}{/tex} .... (v) From Eqs. (i) and (v), we find that i 1 and i 2 are out of phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Now, i 2 + i 1 {tex}=\frac{V_{m} \sin \omega t}{R}+\frac{V_{m} \cos \omega t}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{V_{m}}{R}=A \cos \phi{/tex} and {tex}\frac{V_{m}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}=A \sin \phi{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad i_{1}+i_{2}=A \cos \phi \sin \omega t+A \sin \phi \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}=A \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} Where {tex}A=\sqrt{(A \cos \phi)^{2}+(A \sin \phi)^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{B}{A} C=\left[\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{R^{2}}+\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} and {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{R}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}{/tex} Hence, i = i 1 + i 2 {tex}=\left[\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{R^{2}}+\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} or {tex}\frac{i}{V_{m}}=\frac{1}{Z}=\left[\frac{1}{R^{2}}+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} This is the expression for impedance Z of the circuit. Hence these are the required results.

According to the above figure, the total current 'i' is divided into two parts i2 through R and i1 through a series combination of C and L.
So, we get i = i1 + i2
As, {tex}V_{m} \sin \omega t=R i_{2},{/tex} [from the circuit diagram, we find that]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad i_{2}=\frac{V_{m} \sin \omega t}{R}{/tex} .... (i)
Let q1  is charge on the capacitor at any time t, i1 is the current in the lower circuit.
thus, by Applying KVL in the lower circuit as shown in the figure,

{tex}V_{m} \sin \omega t-\frac{q_{1}}{C}-\frac{L d i_{1}}{d t}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{q_{1}}{C}+\frac{L d^{2} q_{1}}{d t^{2}}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} ..... (ii)
Let {tex}q_{1}=q_{m} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} .... (iii)
{tex}\therefore \quad i_{1}=\frac{d q_{1}}{d t}=q_{m} \omega \cos ^{}(\omega t+\phi){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\left(i_{1}\right)}{d t}=\frac{d^{2} q_{1}}{d t^{2}}=-q_{m} \omega^{2} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex}
Now putting these values in eq. (ii), we get
{tex}q_{m}\left[\frac{1}{C}+L\left(-\omega^{2}\right)\right] \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}
If {tex}\phi=0{/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{1}{C}-L \omega^{2}\right)>0{/tex}
then {tex}q_{m}=\frac{V_{m}}{\left(\frac{1}{C}-L \omega^{2}\right)}{/tex} .... (iv)
From Eq. (iii), {tex}i_{1}=\frac{d q_{1}}{d t}=\omega q_{m} \cos (\omega t+\phi){/tex}
Using eq. (iv), {tex}i_{1}=\frac{\omega V_{m} \cos (\omega t+\phi)}{\frac{1}{C}-L \omega^{2}}{/tex}
Taking {tex}\phi=0 ; i_{1}=\frac{V_{m} \cos (\omega t)}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}{/tex} .... (v)
From Eqs. (i) and (v), we find that i1 and i2 are out of phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Now, i2 + i1 {tex}=\frac{V_{m} \sin \omega t}{R}+\frac{V_{m} \cos \omega t}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}{/tex}
Let {tex}\frac{V_{m}}{R}=A \cos \phi{/tex} and {tex}\frac{V_{m}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}=A \sin \phi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad i_{1}+i_{2}=A \cos \phi \sin \omega t+A \sin \phi \cos \omega t{/tex}
{tex}=A \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex}
Where {tex}A=\sqrt{(A \cos \phi)^{2}+(A \sin \phi)^{2}}{/tex}
and {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{B}{A} C=\left[\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{R^{2}}+\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
and {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{R}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)}{/tex}
Hence, i = i1 + i2 {tex}=\left[\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{R^{2}}+\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex}
or {tex}\frac{i}{V_{m}}=\frac{1}{Z}=\left[\frac{1}{R^{2}}+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}-L \omega\right)^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
This is the expression for impedance Z of the circuit. Hence these are the required results.

question_text: For an LCR circuit driven at frequency {tex}\omega{/tex} , the equation reads {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t}+R i+\frac{q}{C}=v_{i}=v_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} Multiply the equation by i and simplify where possible. Interpret each term physically. Cast the equation in the form of conservation of energy statement. Integrate the equation over one cycle to find that the phase difference between v and i must be acute.
question_html:
For an LCR circuit driven at frequency {tex}\omega{/tex}, the equation reads {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t}+R i+\frac{q}{C}=v_{i}=v_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}

  1. Multiply the equation by i and simplify where possible.
  2. Interpret each term physically.
  3. Cast the equation in the form of conservation of energy statement.
  4. Integrate the equation over one cycle to find that the phase difference between v and i must be acute.
solution_text: Consider L-C-R series circuit with AC supply {tex}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}{/tex} Applying voltage Kirchhoff's law over the circuit {tex}\therefore V_{L}+V_{C}+V_{R}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t}+R i+\frac{q}{c}=V_{i}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} Multiply the above equation by i on both the sides. {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t}+R i+\frac{q}{c}=V_{i}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} Multiply above equation by {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} on both sides {tex}\frac{1}{2} L i \frac{d i}{d t}+i \frac{q}{2 C}+\frac{i^{2} R}{2}=\frac{1}{2} V_{m} i \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\left(\because i=\frac{d q}{d t}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d\left(\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}\right)}{d t}+\frac{1}{2 C} \frac{d q^{2}}{d t}+\frac{i^{2} R}{2}=\frac{1}{2} V_{m} i \sin \omega t{/tex} ..... (i) {tex}\frac{d\left(\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}\right)}{d t}{/tex} represents the rate of change of potential energy in inductance L. {tex}\frac{d}{d t} \frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Represents energy stored in dt time in the capacitor. I 2 R represents Joules heating loss. {tex}\frac{1}{2} V_{m} i \sin \omega t{/tex} is the rate at which driving force pours in energy. it goes into ohmic loss and increase of stored energy in capacitor and inductor. Here equation (I) is in the form of conservation of energy statement. Integrating eqn. (I) both sides with respect to dt over a cycle we get {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{T} \frac{d\left(\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}\right)}{d t} d t+\int \limits_{0}^{T} \frac{d q^{2}}{d t 2 C} d t{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{T} \frac{i^{2} R}{2} d t=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{T} V_{m} i \sin \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{T} \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}+\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}\right] d t+\frac{1}{2} \int \limits_{0}^{T} i^{2} R d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{T} V i d t\left[\text { because } V=V_{m} \sin \omega t\right]{/tex} {tex}0+\frac{1}{2} i^{2} R T=\frac{1}{2} \int \limits_{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex} {tex}i^{2} R T=\int \limits_{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex} As i 2 RT is +ve [ {tex}\because{/tex} i 2 , R and T can never be negative] So, {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex} is positive which is only possible if phase difference {tex}\phi{/tex} is constant and the angle is acute.
solution_html:
Consider L-C-R series circuit with AC supply
{tex}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}{/tex}
Applying voltage Kirchhoff's law over the circuit
{tex}\therefore V_{L}+V_{C}+V_{R}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}
{tex}L \frac{d i}{d t}+R i+\frac{q}{c}=V_{i}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}

  1. Multiply the above equation by i on both the sides.
    {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t}+R i+\frac{q}{c}=V_{i}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}
    Multiply above equation by {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} on both sides
    {tex}\frac{1}{2} L i \frac{d i}{d t}+i \frac{q}{2 C}+\frac{i^{2} R}{2}=\frac{1}{2} V_{m} i \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\left(\because i=\frac{d q}{d t}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d\left(\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}\right)}{d t}+\frac{1}{2 C} \frac{d q^{2}}{d t}+\frac{i^{2} R}{2}=\frac{1}{2} V_{m} i \sin \omega t{/tex} ..... (i)
    1. {tex}\frac{d\left(\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}\right)}{d t}{/tex} represents the rate of change of potential energy in inductance L.
    2. {tex}\frac{d}{d t} \frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Represents energy stored in dt time in the capacitor.
    3. I2R represents Joules heating loss.
    4. {tex}\frac{1}{2} V_{m} i \sin \omega t{/tex} is the rate at which driving force pours in energy. it goes into ohmic loss and increase of stored energy in capacitor and inductor.
  3. Here equation (I) is in the form of conservation of energy statement.
  4. Integrating eqn. (I) both sides with respect to dt over a cycle we get
    {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{T} \frac{d\left(\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}\right)}{d t} d t+\int \limits_{0}^{T} \frac{d q^{2}}{d t 2 C} d t{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{T} \frac{i^{2} R}{2} d t=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{T} V_{m} i \sin \omega t d t{/tex}
    {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{T} \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}+\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}\right] d t+\frac{1}{2} \int \limits_{0}^{T} i^{2} R d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{T} V i d t\left[\text { because } V=V_{m} \sin \omega t\right]{/tex}
    {tex}0+\frac{1}{2} i^{2} R T=\frac{1}{2} \int \limits_{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex}
    {tex}i^{2} R T=\int \limits_{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex}
    As i2RT is +ve [{tex}\because{/tex} i2, R and T can never be negative]
    So, {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex} is positive which is only possible if phase difference {tex}\phi{/tex} is constant and the angle is acute.

question_text: In the LCR circuit shown in Figure, the ac driving voltage is {tex}v=v_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} Write down the equation of motion for q (t). At t = t 0 , the voltage source stops and R is short-circuited. Now write down how much energy is stored in each of L and C. Describe the subsequent motion of charges.
question_html:
In the LCR circuit shown in Figure, the ac driving voltage is {tex}v=v_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}

  1. Write down the equation of motion for q (t).
  2. At t = t0, the voltage source stops and R is short-circuited. Now write down how much energy is stored in each of L and C.
  3. Describe the subsequent motion of charges.
solution_text: Given {tex}V=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} Let current at any instant be i, Applying KVL in the given circuit {tex}i R+L \frac{d i}{d t}+\frac{q}{C}-V_{m} \sin \omega t=0{/tex} .... (i) Now, we can write {tex}i=\frac{d q}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d i}{d t}=\frac{d^{2} q}{d t^{2}}{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{d q}{d t} R+L \frac{d^{2} q}{d t^{2}}+\frac{q}{C}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L \frac{d^{2} q}{d t^{2}}+R \frac{d q}{d t}+\frac{q}{C}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex} This is the required equation of variation (motion) of charge. Let q {tex}=q_{m} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}=-q_{m} \cos (\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}i=i_{m} \sin (\omega t+\phi)=q_{m} \omega \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}i_{m}=\frac{V_{m}}{Z}=\frac{V_{m^{}}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X_{C}-X_{L}}{R}\right){/tex} When R is short-circuited at t = t 0 , energy is stored in L and C. {tex}U_{L}=\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} L\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{\left(R^{2}+X_{c}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2} \sin ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right){/tex} and {tex}U_{C}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{q^{2}}{C}=\frac{1}{2 C}{/tex} {tex}\left[q^{2} m \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C}\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C} \times\left(\frac{i_{m}}{\omega}\right)^{2} \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{i^{2} m}{2 C \omega^{2}} \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right) \quad\left[\because i_{m}=q_{m} \omega\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C}\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2} \frac{\cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right)}{\omega^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C \omega^{2}}\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2} \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right){/tex} The circuit becomes an L-C oscillator when $R$ is short-circuited. The capacitor will go on discharging and all energy will go to L back and forth. Hence, there is oscillation of energy from electrostatic to magnetic and magnetic to electrostatic.
solution_html:
1. Given {tex}V=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}
    Let current at any instant be i,
    Applying KVL in the given circuit
    {tex}i R+L \frac{d i}{d t}+\frac{q}{C}-V_{m} \sin \omega t=0{/tex} .... (i)
    Now, we can write {tex}i=\frac{d q}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d i}{d t}=\frac{d^{2} q}{d t^{2}}{/tex}
    From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{d q}{d t} R+L \frac{d^{2} q}{d t^{2}}+\frac{q}{C}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L \frac{d^{2} q}{d t^{2}}+R \frac{d q}{d t}+\frac{q}{C}=V_{m} \sin \omega t{/tex}
    This is the required equation of variation (motion) of charge.
  2. Let q {tex}=q_{m} \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex} {tex}=-q_{m} \cos (\omega t+\phi){/tex}
    {tex}i=i_{m} \sin (\omega t+\phi)=q_{m} \omega \sin (\omega t+\phi){/tex}
    {tex}i_{m}=\frac{V_{m}}{Z}=\frac{V_{m^{}}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X_{C}-X_{L}}{R}\right){/tex}
    When R is short-circuited at t = t0, energy is stored in L and C.
    {tex}U_{L}=\frac{1}{2} L i^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} L\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{\left(R^{2}+X_{c}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2} \sin ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right){/tex}
    and {tex}U_{C}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{q^{2}}{C}=\frac{1}{2 C}{/tex}{tex}\left[q^{2} m \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right)\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C}\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C} \times\left(\frac{i_{m}}{\omega}\right)^{2} \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{i^{2} m}{2 C \omega^{2}} \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right) \quad\left[\because i_{m}=q_{m} \omega\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C}\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2} \frac{\cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right)}{\omega^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2 C \omega^{2}}\left[\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}}}\right]^{2} \cos ^{2}\left(\omega t_{0}+\phi\right){/tex}
  3. The circuit becomes an L-C oscillator when $R$ is short-circuited. The capacitor will go on discharging and all energy will go to L back and forth. Hence, there is oscillation of energy from electrostatic to magnetic and magnetic to electrostatic.
